sentences.text: [TONE]
My name is Charles Mudryk.
M-U-D-R-Y-K.
OK.
My name at birth was Aaron.
A-A-R-O-N.
And last name, M-U-D-R-Y-K.
My parents and my brother and sister used to call me Arle.
I am not sure, but I think it's after my uncle.
I'm 60 years old.
Just turned 60 in April.
Dombrowitz.
D-O-M-B-R-O-W-I-T-Z, I believe, phonetically.
The Ukraine.
It used to be-- at one time belong to Poland.
Then it was Ukraine, so I would imagine it was Ukraine when I was born.
Yes.
I remember certain things, like I remember my parents owned a big house, and we had more like a farm area.
Big acreage.
What I remember, I must have been about three years old.
I was sitting in a tree-- [CLEARS THROAT] excuse me-- and I had a coin in my hand.
And I put it in my mouth, and it went in, and it-- I didn't swallow it, and it was stuck in the middle.
And I remember my father carrying me to the doctor.
And-- and I finally threw up, and I threw up the coin.
That was one of the-- I also remember when the Germans came into our town on motorcycles, and I was-- I was fascinated by the motorcycles.
And I would go up there and touch it, and they were very friendly when they first came in.
And I remember that part of our-- our town.
My father used to deal in cattle.
He used to buy and sell cattle and-- and horses and that type of thing.
He and my uncle.
Livestock, right.
[CLEARS THROAT]
My father's name was Avrum, and my mother's name was Chava.
When my sister came over here from Israel-- about three or four years ago, I think it was-- she-- she was telling me that Mudryk in Ukrainian means the wise man.
So I don't know exactly how that came about.
But from what they were saying, that my grandfather was the wise man, and I guess that's how we got the name.
In my immediate family?
A brother.
I have a brother and a sister.
My oldest sister, the oldest one, is-- she's just 68 now.
And my brother must be 64 now.
And their names.
Sarah and David.
I spoke Yiddish.
But right now, my Yiddish isn't too good.
Yiddish, and I must have spoken Ukrainian.
But I don't speak Ukrainian.
I don't remember too much about that.
Yes.
Not in the immediate house.
I guess there was, like, almost, like, a duplex.
And on one side, my aunt and uncle lived, and we lived on the other side.
Right.
A female cousin and a male cousin.
They were Orthodox.
I don't know how religious.
I remember going to the synagogue on Saturdays and holidays.
I remember that.
But as far as the extent of their religion, I don't.
Yes, I remember my mother bentching on Friday night.
[SOB] [DRINKS] [COUGHS]
Yes.
[CLEARS THROAT] Well, I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the ghetto that the Germans sent us.
Right.
Yes.
[CLEARS THROAT] They were-- I think they were anxious and nervous.
I didn't exactly know why, but they were concerned.
And touching them.
Yeah.
They were very friendly when they first came in.
[CLEARS THROAT] They-- they told us we had to move, and they were going to relocate us.
And they moved us, I think, to a different part of town.
And then from there, they were going to-- they were lining us up to take us to-- on the trains, to relocate us.
They wouldn't tell us where.
And as we were marching, my father knew that something was wrong, and he grabbed me and my brother and sister and mother, and we started running.
And they started shooting after us.
No.
We weren't hit, but we could hear the bullets and the firearms going off.
I would say about six months or less.
Somewhere around there.
No, I have no idea whose house that was.
I don't recall.
It was kind of crowded, I think.
But I don't remember too much of that.
Yes, at that point, there was no problem getting food and--
--things like that.
Right.
Dombrowitz, right.
Until we-- the woods.
It was a field, and then the woods began.
So that's where we wound up in.
No.
Once we got into the woods, I don't think they-- they finally gave up.
You know, they weren't going to-- because they were marching up.
There were a lot of people in the lines, so I mean, I don't think they would leave the lines and let the other people escape.
My-- my uncle and cousin-- a male cousin-- ran with us.
But my aunt and her daughter didn't-- didn't want to go.
So they remained with the-- with the rest of them.
They went on the train.
And from there, I imagine, the concentration camps.
But we didn't hear from them.
[LAUGHS]
I don't remember the first night, but I do remember the cold nights where, you know, we tried to keep warm.
And it was difficult.
Used coats or whatever we had to keep warm.
On the ground, [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the open.
Yeah.
No.
When we-- we didn't stay in one place.
We had to move from one area to the other so we wouldn't be detected by the Germans or the Ukrainians.
So sometimes we'd sleep in a barn and-- whenever there was shelter.
We ate-- [LAUGHS] in the summer, I remember-- were the berries.
You had strawberries and blueberries and so on.
And also, my parents or one of the adults would go and-- to a farmer.
And if the farmer was friendly enough, they would give us food.
And that's how my parents got killed.
They went out looking for food.
And from what we understood later on, they were captured by-- by Ukrainians, not the Germans-- and dragged through the street on a horse.
They tied them up until they-- they died.
This-- I guess later on, some of the adults told us.
They found out what had happened.
See, a lot of the Ukrainians would capture Jews and turn them over to the Germans for a reward or something.
They would do it just for the fun of it.
It was a-- a sport to them.
Right.
Well, there were other adults in there.
There was a group maybe of 20 people who lived in the same area with us.
[SIGHS] There may have been several leaders.
I don't think there was only one that would confer when we should move, and what we should do.
Yes, they did.
Well, they would sabotage a lot of time.
My brother and some of the other men would sabotage bridges and so on.
So they did have some weapons with them.
And this was later on during the war.
It didn't happen initially, but later on it happened.
Not too long.
Maybe about a year, I think.
Somewhere around there.
Well, my brother and sister and the other adults--
--would take care of us.
Just to be quiet and don't say anything.
Well, yeah.
We were-- if we heard somebody coming, we would hide.
But sometimes they surprised you and came from a different direction.
And then, you know, we were at their mercy.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Right.
Come to think of it, there weren't-- there's-- there was my cousin before he and his father were killed also.
I don't think there were any other children.
Most of them were adults there.
He was around he was around 14 at the time when he got killed, I think.
He was a little older.
I remember my uncle's name, Asher, but I don't remember the cousin's name.
My mother's brother.
It was Bechek.
[PAPERS RUSTLING]
Well, I remember when the war was over, and-- and they-- they shaved our heads and disinfected us, so to speak.
But I remember having lice and having itching all over the place.
And-- where did we sleep?
Most of the time, we slept outdoors.
But when we moved from place to place, we would sleep in barns. or any-- usually in barns, If we weren't detected.
We had to be careful not to be detected.
But usually when we moved was at night.
So--
I don't remember anybody really getting sick, like having a cold or the flu or anything, which is unusual.
Yeah, yeah.
I don't recall that-- anything like that happening.
Right.
After the war-- or the war had ended, and I went with this one couple, and my brother and sister went with another couple, and we were separated.
And what had happened was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this couple that I was with were communists.
And they were afraid, after the war, that some-- they-- they would capture them, for some reason, and do something to them.
So they-- they left me at a train station, in the city of Rowno, which is the Ukraine.
And they said, we'll be right back.
Well, 10, 15, 20 minutes later, nothing happened, and I was left alone.
So I decided-- it was at night-- I slept in the train station by myself.
And the next morning, I got up, and I was wandering around the city.
The little town.
And I didn't know what to do.
I-- I sat down near a-- a house, and this couple took me in.
Their names were the Achtenbaums.
They were Jewish, and they took me in.
And I lived with them until I came here.
By the Russians.
The Russians came in, and-- and before we could do anything, they-- they made sure that we were clean and so on.
So I remember having to go through--
Yes.
Yeah.
They were the ones with us-- they were in the woods with us.
Evidently, they were running.
They were running for some reason, or being afraid of being captured as being Communist.
I don't under-- quite understand why.
But-- And they just-- just left me alone.
Right, and took me in.
Well, they-- they had their-- they lived in an apartment, I guess it was.
And I lived with them.
And from there, from Rowno, we went to France.
They decided we want-- right.
They decided they weren't going to stay there, and they-- and we moved to France.
And I lived in France from '45 to '48.
Yeah, I remember the train ride.
It was--
It was-- it was-- for a kid, I've never been on a train before.
I think it was a very exciting experience.
They became like-- yeah.
They were very close.
And like I said, I stayed with them until '48, when my aunt brought me over here to Detroit.
Right.
I learned French.
Went to school-- elementary school-- there.
Yes.
Well, no, not really.
In fact, we lived in the Jewish ghetto.
Not a ghetto, but it's mostly Jewish people.
And funny you should ask.
My son just went there last year.
He went to France and Paris and visited at the neighborhood, and it's still predominantly Jewish.
It's been Jewish since the 1800s.
So.
Yes.
She put an ad in the Jewish In the And the Achtenbaums saw the ad, and that's how she got in touch with me.
And they-- they wanted me to come to the United States with my aunt.
I really didn't want to.
I wanted to go and stay with them, because I was more attached to them.
And that's how I came over here.
I sure do.
[LAUGHS]
It was on a-- on a boat called the Ernie Pyle.
And this was in February, and it was a really rough winter.
And I remember going on-- staying-- staying on deck and going up.
And you could see the waves as high as the sky.
And I was-- I was introduced to the candy bar, the Milky Way and the Snickers.
And I was eating candy bars left and right.
And I got so sick, I couldn't-- couldn't look at another candy bar.
And I remember they gave us a medication for sea sickness that tasted like root beer.
And ever since then, I cannot stand the smell of root beer because of the medication [INAUDIBLE].
But that trip on the boat lasted 14 days because of the rough weather.
So I do remember.
I traveled alone, right.
The Achtenbaums brought me on a-- took me to the train in Paris.
And from Paris, we went to Le Havre, which is the port in France.
And from there, we took the boat.
I-- we-- I did for a while, and then the Achtenbaums went to Israel.
And after that, I-- I lost touch with them.
And in fact, recently, about three years ago, Mrs.
Achtenbaum wrote to me and corresponded.
I corresponded a few times, but it's kind of hard to keep in touch when you haven't seen them for so long.
On the boat, right.
[CLEARS THROAT]
I saw the Statue-- the Statue of Liberty.
That was the sign that we were in the United States.
I thought it was great.
[LAUGHS]
My cousin.
My cousin Ida came to pick me up in New York.
And she asked me what name did I want to use.
And I said, my name is Carole.
That was the name that they gave me in France.
And she said, well, do you want another name?
And I started-- I thought, and I said, well, Charles sounds pretty good, so why not pick Charles?
That's how it became Charles.
So you went from--
Aaron to Carole to Charles.
Aliases.
Your cousin came and picked you up in New York.
In New York, right.
We stayed at a hotel.
I don't remember the name of the hotel.
It was-- more like six hours.
We didn't stay very long.
And from there, we went on a train to Detroit.
And she asked me what I wanted to drink on the train.
I said, I'll have a beer.
And she looked at me.
A beer?
I said OK, I'll have milk.
She didn't realize that in France, children drink beer and wine for lunch and dinner without any-- anybody saying anything.
That's part of the culture.
[CHUCKLES]
